query: What's the correct way to disclose and reference AI tools in a paper?

queries: What's the correct way to disclose and reference AI tools in a paper?

retrieved.title: Citing and labelling AI
heading_path: References within the text
retrieved.text: for example, be presented in a table and document page by page which AI tool was used and how. Alternatively, a single reference to collaborative AI use, e.g. in the introduction or in the methods section, may be sufficient to create transparency without disproportionately increasing the documentation effort. The crucial factor is that your own contribution is clearly recognisable and the results remain scientifically verifiable. Example: In the preparation of this thesis, AI-supported applications were used as auxiliary tools. ChatGPT (OpenAI) was employed to develop linguistic variants, revise draft texts stylistically, refine formulations, support the structure of individual sections and improve the overall readability of passages. In addition, it provided assistance with Excel, for example by suggesting suitable formulae for the quantitative analysis of contributions and categories. ChatGPT was also used to produce draft translations of Italian and French texts and to convert text from non-machine-readable scans into editable and searchable formats. DeepL was used in addition, both for translations and for the linguistic optimisation of translation drafts produced with ChatGPT, thereby further improving the accuracy and stylistic quality of the texts. Finally, Transkribus was used for the transcription of historical documents written in Kurrent script. All of the aforementioned applications were used exclusively in a supportive capacity and in agreement with the thesis supervisor. Their use was in accordance with the applicable AI guidelines of the University of Vienna

retrieved.title: Citing and labelling AI
retrieved.text:
In order to ensure the visibility of your independent contribution, it is essential to present your work transparently and in a manner that allows for clear and accurate tracking of the use of AI. It is crucial to clearly distinguish the parts of the work that originate from you, ensuring that your independent performance is accurately assessed. Failure to disclose the use of AI could be considered a breach of the principles of good academic practice.

The responsibility for determining how the use of AI is to be made transparent lies with the respective lecturer, the study programme director (SPL) or the respective department. In the absence of specific requirements, established best practices may serve as a useful guideline.

A distinction is made between references within the text and entries in the bibliography and, secondly, between content generated entirely by AI and content produced with the assistance of AI.

retrieved.title: Citing and labelling AI
heading_path: References within the text
retrieved.text: With regard to references within the text, a distinction is made between the literal reproduction of AI-generated content and the collaborative development of content with the help of AI. #### Literal reproduction If AI-generated content is incorporated into the text verbatim, this may be treated as a conventional quotation. Traceability is ensured if, in addition to the quoted text,it is recommended to indicate - the name of the AI-tool and, where applicable, the company behind it - the model used - the year of use - and the prompt that generated the text Example: Text generated by ChatGPT (OpenAI, model o1, 2024. Prompt: “Explain the fundamental principles of good academic practice in one sentence.”) In this case, the prompt documents your contribution by indicating which instruction was given. In practice, however, AI output often is created in an iterative process involving multiple prompts and queries. #### Collaborative development Complete documentation of all individual AI interactions is generally not practical. What is more important is that it is clear how AI was used in principle and that the writer was responsible for controlling, selecting and revising the content. The documentation can, for example, be presented in a table and document page by page which AI tool was used and how. Alternatively, a single reference to collaborative AI use, e.g. in the introduction or in the methods section, may be sufficient to

retrieved.title: Citing and labelling AI
heading_path: References in the bibliography
retrieved.text:
AI tools are not considered traditional sources, as interaction with them, similar to personal conversations, is not reproducible or openly accessible.

Reference only with publicly accessible link

According to international citation standards, AI tools should therefore only be included in a bibliography or reference list if a public URL to the respective chat can be provided. Although tools such as ChatGPT generate unique URLs for each conversation, these are generally accessible only with the user’s login credentials and are therefore not available to third parties ([The Chicago Manual of Stile Online](https://www.chicagomanualofstyle.org/qanda/data/faq/topics/Documentation/faq0422.html)).

retrieved.title: AI in academic writing
heading_path: Use Cases of AI in scientific writing
retrieved.text:
AI tools can support you at different stages of academic writing. It is important to use AI as a tool within the writing process and not as a substitute for your own subject-specific engagement. How AI may support your writing depends on the stage of writing you are currently in.

Important: Do not use generative AI tools such as ChatGPT or u:ai for research. These tools do not access databases and may fabricate sources. Instead, use dedicated literature research tools such as Elicit, Consensus or others.

The library offers specialised training sessions on [literature research with AI](http://urise.univie.ac.at/course/index.php?categoryid=6&wbtsearch=ai+literacy).

retrieved.title: Citing and labelling AI
heading_path: References within the text
retrieved.text: historical documents written in Kurrent script. All of the aforementioned applications were used exclusively in a supportive capacity and in agreement with the thesis supervisor. Their use was in accordance with the applicable AI guidelines of the University of Vienna and with due regard for academic independence: they served to facilitate linguistic, technical and practical work steps and did not replace independent academic work. All results constituted solely technical or linguistic support and were critically reviewed, adapted and contextualised by the author. The selection, analysis and interpretation of sources, the development of the argument, the substantive evaluation of the data and the final drafting of the text were carried out independently. The author bears sole responsibility for all content, translations and results of this thesis. The use of AI-supported tools is therefore to be understood not as a delegation of tasks, but as a reflective complement within a collaborative working process. In particular in the case of theses, be sure to clarify with your supervisor how the use of AI is to be documented.

retrieved.title: Checklist for the responsible use of AI
heading_path: Checklist for the responsible use of AI
retrieved.text: - Bear in mind that texts and documents you enter into AI may themselves be copyrighted: therefore, ensure that your input is not used for AI training. Honesty & Independence - Do not pass off AI-generated content as your own work - Differentiate between Higher Order Concerns (HOCs) and Later Order Concerns (LOCs) – depending on the learning outcomes. Transparency & traceability - Document which AI tools you have used, including the version. - Disclose the purpose and scope of the AI's use. - If AI-generated content has been copied verbatim, specify the prompt used. - Check whether detailed documentation of all prompts is necessary or practical. - If you used AI in a collaborative process, document how it contributed to idea development. - Only include AI tools in the bibliography if there is a publicly accessible link to the chat. Using AI as a reflection aid - Use AI as a Socratic dialogue partner that asks questions and provides food for thought. - Allow AI to assist you in developing research questions or argumentation structures, without outsourcing your intellectual performance. Clear distinction between AI support and your own contribution - Edit and review AI-generated content so that your own scientific voice remains recognisable. - Ensure that AI only supports the scientific writing process, but does not replace it.

retrieved.title: AI in academic writing
heading_path: Use Cases of AI in scientific writing
Prompting tip: How to formulate prompts that ensure your own contribution
retrieved.text: need to be corrected and why. Then decide for yourself whether and how you want to accept the suggested corrections. The key is: carefully review all suggestions to maintain technical accuracy and your own writing style. Do not uncritically accept all corrections or comments suggested by the AI. Suitable tools: ChatGPT, DeepL Write, Grammarly AI can support the writing process at all stages. In tasks that relate to content-related argumentation, interpretation or evaluation, it is essential to use AI with restraint and reflection.

retrieved.title: AI and Exams
heading_path: Examinations with AI: What does apply here?
retrieved.text: permitted is prohibited. If you are unsure, you should always consult your lecturers in order to clarify expectations and framework conditions at an early stage. - Transparency and documentation: If you are permitted to use AI tools for written assignments, you must [fully disclose](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/academic-work-and-writing-with-ai/citing-and-labelling-ai/#c1269189) such use in accordance with the principles of good academic practice. - Examination formats are changing: You should be prepared for greater emphasis on competences such as reflection and transfer. To ensure the independence of your performance, written assignments may be supplemented by oral components, plausibility checks or grade-relevant discussions. - Personal responsibility: It is imperative that you, the author, accept full responsibility for the accuracy, academic validity and integrity of the results submitted. As language models are not "knowledge machines" and may produce [hallucinations](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/what-is-artificial-intelligence/#c1268979), it is essential that all AI-generated content is critically reviewed. - Consequences of misconduct: The use of unauthorised AI tools constitutes academic misconduct. This results in a non-assessment (entry of an “X”) or the annulment of a grade that has already been awarded. In serious cases, the Rectorate may impose exclusion from studies for up to two semesters.

retrieved.title: AI in academic writing
heading_path: Use Cases of AI in scientific writing
Prompting tip: How to formulate prompts that ensure your own contribution
retrieved.text: that the AI supports you with organizational tasks, such as clustering topics or summarizing abstracts. The request should make it clear that the selection, evaluation, and classification of the literature is done independently and that the AI does not make any decisions regarding relevance. Please note the information on [literature research](#c1268854). Even during the writing process, AI should be used in such a way that it provides guidance and feedback without taking over key decisions regarding content or lines of argumentation, or formulating text for you. - Revision: Language and formal precision During the revision phase, AI can be particularly helpful with linguistic and formal aspects. This support relates to the presentation of the text, not its content. Possible areas of application: - Feedback on language, style, and text context: Formulate your request in such a way that the AI provides feedback on linguistic quality and readability without changing the content or introducing new arguments. Ask it to highlight problematic passages and suggest improvements rather than completely rewriting the text. - Spelling and correction assistance: Ensure that the request relates exclusively to orthographic correctness. The AI should correct spelling mistakes, typos, or obvious formal inaccuracies without changing the sentence structure or meaning. Tip: Don't let the AI formulate a corrected version of the text; instead, ask for a list of points to be corrected. Then you can decide for yourself

retrieved.title: AI in academic writing
heading_path: Use Cases of AI in scientific writing
Prompting tip: How to formulate prompts that ensure your own contribution
retrieved.text: unclear or fragmentary passages may remain visible. Tip: You can also have AI tools read texts aloud to you (e.g., Textreader, Pickles). - During writing: Structure and feedback During the writing process, AI can be used for structuring and providing selective feedback on text passages. Possible areas of application: - Suggestions for outlines or tables of contents: Formulate your request in such a way that the AI shows several possible structural variants and explains their internal logic. Avoid requests for a finished or optimal outline. - Tips for ensuring individual paragraphs are easy to understand: Ask the AI to highlight any ambiguities, long sentence structures, or potential obstacles to comprehension. The request should be aimed at obtaining feedback on readability, not at rephrasing or expanding content. - Feedback on text context or argumentation logic: Structure your request in such a way that the AI highlights breaks, jumps, or missing transitions in the text. Avoid requests that require restructuring or adding content arguments. The goal is to review your own argumentation, not to have it developed by the AI. - Support with literature overviews: Formulate your request in such a way that the AI supports you with organizational tasks, such as clustering topics or summarizing abstracts. The request should make it clear that the selection, evaluation, and classification of the literature is done independently and that the AI does not make

retrieved.title: What is Artificial Intelligence?
heading_path: Risks associated with the use of AI
What implications does this have for the use of AI in my Studies?
retrieved.text:
- It is important to note that generarive tools such as ChatGPT are not intended for use as research tools and should not be relied upon as reliable sources of knowledge.
- They cannot substitute specialist literature and may also provide incorrect and inaccurate information.
- They may continue, rephrase or summarise texts, but there is no guarantee that they are factually correct.
- Risk of plagiarism: Due to the way AI tools generate text, existing content may be reproduced without alteration or only slightly modified without citing sources.

It is imperative that all statements made with the assistance of AI are meticulously reviewed and verified using reliable sources. Students must assume full responsibility for the academic integrity of their work, including its content, arguments and results.

retrieved.title: AI in academic writing
heading_path: Use Cases of AI in scientific writing
Prompting tip: How to formulate prompts that ensure your own contribution
retrieved.text: typos, or obvious formal inaccuracies without changing the sentence structure or meaning. Tip: Don't let the AI formulate a corrected version of the text; instead, ask for a list of points to be corrected. Then you can decide for yourself whether and how to accept the suggested corrections. - Word choice and phrasing assistance: AI can assist you with word choice and phrasing. Specifically, AI can help you with suggestions for synonyms, rephrasing, or suggestions for variations of a sentence with different linguistic registers. Structure your query so that the AI suggests alternative expressions or synonyms that are stylistically and technically appropriate. Avoid queries that could lead to simplifications in content or shifts in meaning. The decision on which wording to use remains yours. - Check grammar and punctuation: Ask the AI to identify grammatical errors and punctuation mistakes. The request should make it clear that no changes are to be made to the content and that the technical meaning remains unchanged. #### #### Tip: Lists instead of finished text Don't let the AI formulate a corrected version of the text; instead, ask for a list of the points that need to be corrected and why. Then decide for yourself whether and how you want to accept the suggested corrections. The key is: carefully review all suggestions to maintain technical accuracy and your own writing style. Do not uncritically accept

retrieved.title: AI in academic writing
heading_path: Use Cases of AI in scientific writing
Always follow the teacher's instructions!
retrieved.text: In the context of academic writing, it is the responsibility of the lecturer to determine the permitted use of aids. Any tools that are not explicitly permitted are prohibited. Prior to commencing work on your text, it is essential to familiarise yourself with the documentation requirements for the use of text-generating AI and other aids. It is imperative to take [data protection considerations](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/ai-in-your-studies-what-is-permitted/legal-requirements-data-protection-and-copyright/#c1269046) into account at all times.

retrieved.title: AI in academic writing
heading_path: Use Cases of AI in scientific writing
Prompting tip: How to formulate prompts that ensure your own contribution
retrieved.text: thought-provoking ideas without evaluating or selecting content. Ask for brief explanations of the respective perspectives, not for detailed arguments or text passages. - Develop and narrow down topic suggestions: Ensure that the AI highlights possible variations and distinctions rather than specifying a topic. The request should make it clear that the decision regarding focus, scope, and relevance is yours. - Create suggestions for research questions: Ask the AI to suggest several possible questions and reveal their implicit assumptions. Avoid asking for “the best” or “most suitable” research question, as scientific evaluation must be carried out independently. - Help develop arguments (e.g., in the form of a [Socratic dialogue](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/academic-work-and-writing-with-ai/questions-instead-of-answers-the-socratic-dialogue/)): Formulate your request in such a way that the AI asks questions, identifies counterarguments, or points out possible weaknesses without formulating a line of argumentation itself or suggesting conclusions. - translate foreign-language texts - Convert your own spoken thoughts into text (e.g., using the dictation function from Microsoft or Google, [Whisper Wizard](https://www.whisperwizard.com/)): Make sure that the AI processes your spoken thoughts in a structured and linguistically organized manner, but does not add to the content. The request should make it clear that unclear or fragmentary passages may remain visible. Tip: You can also have AI tools read texts aloud to you (e.g., Textreader, Pickles). - During writing: Structure and feedback During the writing process, AI can be used for structuring and providing

retrieved.title: Maintaining independence: AI-generated or AI-supported?
heading_path: AI as a Tool
retrieved.text: For a text produced with the assistance of AI to qualify as your own work, there must be a substantial human contribution that decisively shapes the form of the generated text. In this case, the AI tool merely serves in a supportive capacity as an executing instrument. This applies: - where a highly sophisticated prompt is used, which you have created yourself and whose formulation may require relevant knowledge; - where text is produced through the combination of strongly directive prompts that determine the essential structural decisions rather than leaving them to the AI tool; - where users further develop the generated text as a stimulus for reflection (cf. [Salden und Leschke 2023](https://hss-opus.ub.ruhr-uni-bochum.de/opus4/frontdoor/deliver/index/docId/9734/file/2023_03_06_Didaktik_Recht_KI_Hochschulbildung.pdf)) In these cases, AI complements the academic working process without replacing your independent contribution. What is crucial is that the substantive decisions remain yours. This is the case, for example, when you use AI: - to generate ideas or as a stimulus for reflection; - to obtain structural or outline suggestions that form the basis for further development (not for direct 1:1 adoption); - as a tool for reflection, for example to review arguments; - or to revise the language of your own texts. It is imperative that you critically examine, develop and embed AI-generated results within your own line of argument. The more actively you guide AI and the more decisions you take yourself, the clearer

retrieved.title: AI and Exams
heading_path: Important information for learning with AI
retrieved.text: The use of AI tools can support the learning process, but it also presents challenges. In order to use AI sensibly and responsibly, it is important to consider a few fundamental aspects. - Due to its [functionality](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/what-is-artificial-intelligence/#c1268970), AI may produce incorrect, incomplete or imprecise information, even if it sounds plausible. Therefore, it is advisable to always compare responses with your own materials. - Subject-specific terminology may be used differently depending on the discipline. When asked to define technical terms, AI generally draws on the meaning most frequently represented in its training data. This may result in definitions that do not correspond to the specific discipline or that lack subject-specific nuances. Only adopt definitions or explanations if they align with the academic requirements of your course. - AI-supported learning tools can heavily structure the learning process, for example through predefined learning paths, automatic revision or recommended content. While this can be helpful, it also entails the risk that your learning process becomes overly externally directed: learning is individual and not every recommendation generated by AI suits your personal learning style. Therefore, consciously set your own priorities. - A common risk factor is increasing dependence on AI tools. If explanations, solution strategies or summaries are regularly delegated to AI, this may impair your ability to think through content independently and to solve problems on your own. Especially at the beginning of your

retrieved.title: Further information
retrieved.text:
Answers to questions about the use of AI in research can be found in the library's services:

- Library courses on AI: How can I use AI tools in my research? What tools are available for literature searches? How can I use such tools effectively? The library offers workshops with different focuses in the series ["AI Literacy - KI-Kompetenz für Studierende"](https://urise.univie.ac.at/course/index.php?categoryid=6&wbtsearch=ai+literacy).
- Library materials for the workshop "AI, but how – effective prompting and research?" [Slides, working materials and further links to the workshop](https://padlet.com/annakrenn4/ai-literacy-ki-kompetenz-f-r-studierende-ai-literacy-ai-comp-tk3fa216fnjk5c22)
- Library materials for the workshop "AI literature search tools" [Slides, working materials and further links to the workshop](https://padlet.com/lillinemetz/ki-literaturrecherche-tools-ai-literacy-2-8ocvkv7s7tn0a5co)

Information about the Computer Center's AI services can be found on the following pages:

- [u:ai – AI-Software](https://zid.univie.ac.at/en/uai)
- [AI-Services of the Computer Center](https://zid.univie.ac.at/en/ai/ai-services-of-the-zid)

- [University of Vienna guidelines on the use of AI in teaching](https://phaidra.univie.ac.at/detail/o:2092606) (in German)

retrieved.title: AI in academic writing
heading_path: Why academic writing?
Areas of application for AI along higher and later order concerns
retrieved.text: # | Concern | Area | Possible Uses | Guidance for Implementation | | --- | --- | --- | --- | | Higher Order Concerns | Structure and organisation | Support in developing logical argumentative structures and organising content | The proposed structure serves as a stimulus, but must be critically examined and independently adapted to the specific requirements of the assignment. | | | Generation of ideas | Inspiration for research questions, theses/hypotheses or perspectives | Ideas must be critically reflected upon, assessed for their academic relevance and selected accordingly. No 1:1 adoption. | | | Literature research | Efficient search and preselection of relevant studies. | Independent engagement with the identified sources remains indispensable. Note: AI research tools often have access only to limited datasets and generally not to library catalogues. | | | Text comprehension | Support with questions of understanding and contextualisation | Intellectual depth must be preserved. Critically assess whether the AI-supported analysis meets academic standards. | | Later Order Concerns | Text revision | Checking spelling, grammar and style | Suggested revisions should be reviewed to ensure that they do not distort the academic context or your individual writing style. | | | Linguistic simplification | Alternative formulations and simplification of complex matters | Ensure that simplified texts remain precise and academically accurate. | | | Literature research | Efficient management and organisation

retrieved.title: Using AI in Your Studies
retrieved.text: and writing](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/academic-work-and-writing-with-ai/) [### AI and examinations](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/ai-and-exams/) [## u:ai – The University of Vienna's AI tool](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/uai-the-university-of-viennas-ai-tool/) [### Further information](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/further-information/) Generative AI (OpenAI, ChatGPT 5.2) was used on this website and its subpages to adapt content for the target audience. It was used in particular to shorten extensive texts and to simplify language, making content easier to understand and more accessible. The AI was used as a supporting tool, while content responsibility and quality review remained with the authors. The use of AI is based on the [Guidelines on AI in Studies and Teaching](https://doi.org/10.25365/phaidra.544), which aim to ensure a responsible and transparent use of AI applications in an academic context.

In serious cases, the Rectorate may impose exclusion from studies for up to two semesters.

reranked.title: AI in academic writing
heading_path: Use Cases of AI in scientific writing
Prompting tip: How to formulate prompts that ensure your own contribution
reranked.text: that the AI supports you with organizational tasks, such as clustering topics or summarizing abstracts. The request should make it clear that the selection, evaluation, and classification of the literature is done independently and that the AI does not make any decisions regarding relevance. Please note the information on [literature research](#c1268854). Even during the writing process, AI should be used in such a way that it provides guidance and feedback without taking over key decisions regarding content or lines of argumentation, or formulating text for you. - Revision: Language and formal precision During the revision phase, AI can be particularly helpful with linguistic and formal aspects. This support relates to the presentation of the text, not its content. Possible areas of application: - Feedback on language, style, and text context: Formulate your request in such a way that the AI provides feedback on linguistic quality and readability without changing the content or introducing new arguments. Ask it to highlight problematic passages and suggest improvements rather than completely rewriting the text. - Spelling and correction assistance: Ensure that the request relates exclusively to orthographic correctness. The AI should correct spelling mistakes, typos, or obvious formal inaccuracies without changing the sentence structure or meaning. Tip: Don't let the AI formulate a corrected version of the text; instead, ask for a list of points to be corrected. Then you can decide for yourself

reranked.title: AI in academic writing
heading_path: Use Cases of AI in scientific writing
Prompting tip: How to formulate prompts that ensure your own contribution
reranked.text: unclear or fragmentary passages may remain visible. Tip: You can also have AI tools read texts aloud to you (e.g., Textreader, Pickles). - During writing: Structure and feedback During the writing process, AI can be used for structuring and providing selective feedback on text passages. Possible areas of application: - Suggestions for outlines or tables of contents: Formulate your request in such a way that the AI shows several possible structural variants and explains their internal logic. Avoid requests for a finished or optimal outline. - Tips for ensuring individual paragraphs are easy to understand: Ask the AI to highlight any ambiguities, long sentence structures, or potential obstacles to comprehension. The request should be aimed at obtaining feedback on readability, not at rephrasing or expanding content. - Feedback on text context or argumentation logic: Structure your request in such a way that the AI highlights breaks, jumps, or missing transitions in the text. Avoid requests that require restructuring or adding content arguments. The goal is to review your own argumentation, not to have it developed by the AI. - Support with literature overviews: Formulate your request in such a way that the AI supports you with organizational tasks, such as clustering topics or summarizing abstracts. The request should make it clear that the selection, evaluation, and classification of the literature is done independently and that the AI does not make

reranked.title: What is Artificial Intelligence?
heading_path: Risks associated with the use of AI
What implications does this have for the use of AI in my Studies?
reranked.text:
- It is important to note that generarive tools such as ChatGPT are not intended for use as research tools and should not be relied upon as reliable sources of knowledge.
- They cannot substitute specialist literature and may also provide incorrect and inaccurate information.
- They may continue, rephrase or summarise texts, but there is no guarantee that they are factually correct.
- Risk of plagiarism: Due to the way AI tools generate text, existing content may be reproduced without alteration or only slightly modified without citing sources.

It is imperative that all statements made with the assistance of AI are meticulously reviewed and verified using reliable sources. Students must assume full responsibility for the academic integrity of their work, including its content, arguments and results.

reranked.title: AI in academic writing
heading_path: Use Cases of AI in scientific writing
Prompting tip: How to formulate prompts that ensure your own contribution
reranked.text: typos, or obvious formal inaccuracies without changing the sentence structure or meaning. Tip: Don't let the AI formulate a corrected version of the text; instead, ask for a list of points to be corrected. Then you can decide for yourself whether and how to accept the suggested corrections. - Word choice and phrasing assistance: AI can assist you with word choice and phrasing. Specifically, AI can help you with suggestions for synonyms, rephrasing, or suggestions for variations of a sentence with different linguistic registers. Structure your query so that the AI suggests alternative expressions or synonyms that are stylistically and technically appropriate. Avoid queries that could lead to simplifications in content or shifts in meaning. The decision on which wording to use remains yours. - Check grammar and punctuation: Ask the AI to identify grammatical errors and punctuation mistakes. The request should make it clear that no changes are to be made to the content and that the technical meaning remains unchanged. #### #### Tip: Lists instead of finished text Don't let the AI formulate a corrected version of the text; instead, ask for a list of the points that need to be corrected and why. Then decide for yourself whether and how you want to accept the suggested corrections. The key is: carefully review all suggestions to maintain technical accuracy and your own writing style. Do not uncritically accept

reranked.title: AI in academic writing
heading_path: Use Cases of AI in scientific writing
Always follow the teacher's instructions!
reranked.text: In the context of academic writing, it is the responsibility of the lecturer to determine the permitted use of aids. Any tools that are not explicitly permitted are prohibited. Prior to commencing work on your text, it is essential to familiarise yourself with the documentation requirements for the use of text-generating AI and other aids. It is imperative to take [data protection considerations](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/ai-in-your-studies-what-is-permitted/legal-requirements-data-protection-and-copyright/#c1269046) into account at all times.

reranked.title: AI in academic writing
heading_path: Use Cases of AI in scientific writing
Prompting tip: How to formulate prompts that ensure your own contribution
reranked.text: thought-provoking ideas without evaluating or selecting content. Ask for brief explanations of the respective perspectives, not for detailed arguments or text passages. - Develop and narrow down topic suggestions: Ensure that the AI highlights possible variations and distinctions rather than specifying a topic. The request should make it clear that the decision regarding focus, scope, and relevance is yours. - Create suggestions for research questions: Ask the AI to suggest several possible questions and reveal their implicit assumptions. Avoid asking for “the best” or “most suitable” research question, as scientific evaluation must be carried out independently. - Help develop arguments (e.g., in the form of a [Socratic dialogue](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/academic-work-and-writing-with-ai/questions-instead-of-answers-the-socratic-dialogue/)): Formulate your request in such a way that the AI asks questions, identifies counterarguments, or points out possible weaknesses without formulating a line of argumentation itself or suggesting conclusions. - translate foreign-language texts - Convert your own spoken thoughts into text (e.g., using the dictation function from Microsoft or Google, [Whisper Wizard](https://www.whisperwizard.com/)): Make sure that the AI processes your spoken thoughts in a structured and linguistically organized manner, but does not add to the content. The request should make it clear that unclear or fragmentary passages may remain visible. Tip: You can also have AI tools read texts aloud to you (e.g., Textreader, Pickles). - During writing: Structure and feedback During the writing process, AI can be used for structuring and providing

reranked.title: Maintaining independence: AI-generated or AI-supported?
heading_path: AI as a Tool
reranked.text: For a text produced with the assistance of AI to qualify as your own work, there must be a substantial human contribution that decisively shapes the form of the generated text. In this case, the AI tool merely serves in a supportive capacity as an executing instrument. This applies: - where a highly sophisticated prompt is used, which you have created yourself and whose formulation may require relevant knowledge; - where text is produced through the combination of strongly directive prompts that determine the essential structural decisions rather than leaving them to the AI tool; - where users further develop the generated text as a stimulus for reflection (cf. [Salden und Leschke 2023](https://hss-opus.ub.ruhr-uni-bochum.de/opus4/frontdoor/deliver/index/docId/9734/file/2023_03_06_Didaktik_Recht_KI_Hochschulbildung.pdf)) In these cases, AI complements the academic working process without replacing your independent contribution. What is crucial is that the substantive decisions remain yours. This is the case, for example, when you use AI: - to generate ideas or as a stimulus for reflection; - to obtain structural or outline suggestions that form the basis for further development (not for direct 1:1 adoption); - as a tool for reflection, for example to review arguments; - or to revise the language of your own texts. It is imperative that you critically examine, develop and embed AI-generated results within your own line of argument. The more actively you guide AI and the more decisions you take yourself, the clearer

reranked.title: AI and Exams
heading_path: Important information for learning with AI
reranked.text: The use of AI tools can support the learning process, but it also presents challenges. In order to use AI sensibly and responsibly, it is important to consider a few fundamental aspects. - Due to its [functionality](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/what-is-artificial-intelligence/#c1268970), AI may produce incorrect, incomplete or imprecise information, even if it sounds plausible. Therefore, it is advisable to always compare responses with your own materials. - Subject-specific terminology may be used differently depending on the discipline. When asked to define technical terms, AI generally draws on the meaning most frequently represented in its training data. This may result in definitions that do not correspond to the specific discipline or that lack subject-specific nuances. Only adopt definitions or explanations if they align with the academic requirements of your course. - AI-supported learning tools can heavily structure the learning process, for example through predefined learning paths, automatic revision or recommended content. While this can be helpful, it also entails the risk that your learning process becomes overly externally directed: learning is individual and not every recommendation generated by AI suits your personal learning style. Therefore, consciously set your own priorities. - A common risk factor is increasing dependence on AI tools. If explanations, solution strategies or summaries are regularly delegated to AI, this may impair your ability to think through content independently and to solve problems on your own. Especially at the beginning of your

reranked.title: Further information
reranked.text:
Answers to questions about the use of AI in research can be found in the library's services:

- Library courses on AI: How can I use AI tools in my research? What tools are available for literature searches? How can I use such tools effectively? The library offers workshops with different focuses in the series ["AI Literacy - KI-Kompetenz für Studierende"](https://urise.univie.ac.at/course/index.php?categoryid=6&wbtsearch=ai+literacy).
- Library materials for the workshop "AI, but how – effective prompting and research?" [Slides, working materials and further links to the workshop](https://padlet.com/annakrenn4/ai-literacy-ki-kompetenz-f-r-studierende-ai-literacy-ai-comp-tk3fa216fnjk5c22)
- Library materials for the workshop "AI literature search tools" [Slides, working materials and further links to the workshop](https://padlet.com/lillinemetz/ki-literaturrecherche-tools-ai-literacy-2-8ocvkv7s7tn0a5co)

Information about the Computer Center's AI services can be found on the following pages:

- [u:ai – AI-Software](https://zid.univie.ac.at/en/uai)
- [AI-Services of the Computer Center](https://zid.univie.ac.at/en/ai/ai-services-of-the-zid)

- [University of Vienna guidelines on the use of AI in teaching](https://phaidra.univie.ac.at/detail/o:2092606) (in German)

reranked.title: AI in academic writing
heading_path: Why academic writing?
Areas of application for AI along higher and later order concerns
reranked.text: # | Concern | Area | Possible Uses | Guidance for Implementation | | --- | --- | --- | --- | | Higher Order Concerns | Structure and organisation | Support in developing logical argumentative structures and organising content | The proposed structure serves as a stimulus, but must be critically examined and independently adapted to the specific requirements of the assignment. | | | Generation of ideas | Inspiration for research questions, theses/hypotheses or perspectives | Ideas must be critically reflected upon, assessed for their academic relevance and selected accordingly. No 1:1 adoption. | | | Literature research | Efficient search and preselection of relevant studies. | Independent engagement with the identified sources remains indispensable. Note: AI research tools often have access only to limited datasets and generally not to library catalogues. | | | Text comprehension | Support with questions of understanding and contextualisation | Intellectual depth must be preserved. Critically assess whether the AI-supported analysis meets academic standards. | | Later Order Concerns | Text revision | Checking spelling, grammar and style | Suggested revisions should be reviewed to ensure that they do not distort the academic context or your individual writing style. | | | Linguistic simplification | Alternative formulations and simplification of complex matters | Ensure that simplified texts remain precise and academically accurate. | | | Literature research | Efficient management and organisation

reranked.title: Using AI in Your Studies
reranked.text: and writing](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/academic-work-and-writing-with-ai/) [### AI and examinations](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/ai-and-exams/) [## u:ai – The University of Vienna's AI tool](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/uai-the-university-of-viennas-ai-tool/) [### Further information](/en/using-ai-in-your-studies/further-information/) Generative AI (OpenAI, ChatGPT 5.2) was used on this website and its subpages to adapt content for the target audience. It was used in particular to shorten extensive texts and to simplify language, making content easier to understand and more accessible. The AI was used as a supporting tool, while content responsibility and quality review remained with the authors. The use of AI is based on the [Guidelines on AI in Studies and Teaching](https://doi.org/10.25365/phaidra.544), which aim to ensure a responsible and transparent use of AI applications in an academic context.

selected.title: Citing and labelling AI
heading_path: References within the text
selected.text: for example, be presented in a table and document page by page which AI tool was used and how. Alternatively, a single reference to collaborative AI use, e.g. in the introduction or in the methods section, may be sufficient to create transparency without disproportionately increasing the documentation effort. The crucial factor is that your own contribution is clearly recognisable and the results remain scientifically verifiable. Example: In the preparation of this thesis, AI-supported applications were used as auxiliary tools. ChatGPT (OpenAI) was employed to develop linguistic variants, revise draft texts stylistically, refine formulations, support the structure of individual sections and improve the overall readability of passages. In addition, it provided assistance with Excel, for example by suggesting suitable formulae for the quantitative analysis of contributions and categories. ChatGPT was also used to produce draft translations of Italian and French texts and to convert text from non-machine-readable scans into editable and searchable formats. DeepL was used in addition, both for translations and for the linguistic optimisation of translation drafts produced with ChatGPT, thereby further improving the accuracy and stylistic quality of the texts. Finally, Transkribus was used for the transcription of historical documents written in Kurrent script. All of the aforementioned applications were used exclusively in a supportive capacity and in agreement with the thesis supervisor. Their use was in accordance with the applicable AI guidelines of the University of Vienna

selected.title: Citing and labelling AI
selected.text:
In order to ensure the visibility of your independent contribution, it is essential to present your work transparently and in a manner that allows for clear and accurate tracking of the use of AI. It is crucial to clearly distinguish the parts of the work that originate from you, ensuring that your independent performance is accurately assessed. Failure to disclose the use of AI could be considered a breach of the principles of good academic practice.

The responsibility for determining how the use of AI is to be made transparent lies with the respective lecturer, the study programme director (SPL) or the respective department. In the absence of specific requirements, established best practices may serve as a useful guideline.

A distinction is made between references within the text and entries in the bibliography and, secondly, between content generated entirely by AI and content produced with the assistance of AI.

selected.title: Citing and labelling AI
heading_path: References within the text
selected.text: With regard to references within the text, a distinction is made between the literal reproduction of AI-generated content and the collaborative development of content with the help of AI. #### Literal reproduction If AI-generated content is incorporated into the text verbatim, this may be treated as a conventional quotation. Traceability is ensured if, in addition to the quoted text,it is recommended to indicate - the name of the AI-tool and, where applicable, the company behind it - the model used - the year of use - and the prompt that generated the text Example: Text generated by ChatGPT (OpenAI, model o1, 2024. Prompt: “Explain the fundamental principles of good academic practice in one sentence.”) In this case, the prompt documents your contribution by indicating which instruction was given. In practice, however, AI output often is created in an iterative process involving multiple prompts and queries. #### Collaborative development Complete documentation of all individual AI interactions is generally not practical. What is more important is that it is clear how AI was used in principle and that the writer was responsible for controlling, selecting and revising the content. The documentation can, for example, be presented in a table and document page by page which AI tool was used and how. Alternatively, a single reference to collaborative AI use, e.g. in the introduction or in the methods section, may be sufficient to

selected.title: Citing and labelling AI
heading_path: References in the bibliography
selected.text:
AI tools are not considered traditional sources, as interaction with them, similar to personal conversations, is not reproducible or openly accessible.

Reference only with publicly accessible link

According to international citation standards, AI tools should therefore only be included in a bibliography or reference list if a public URL to the respective chat can be provided. Although tools such as ChatGPT generate unique URLs for each conversation, these are generally accessible only with the user’s login credentials and are therefore not available to third parties ([The Chicago Manual of Stile Online](https://www.chicagomanualofstyle.org/qanda/data/faq/topics/Documentation/faq0422.html)).

selected.title: AI in academic writing
heading_path: Use Cases of AI in scientific writing
selected.text:
AI tools can support you at different stages of academic writing. It is important to use AI as a tool within the writing process and not as a substitute for your own subject-specific engagement. How AI may support your writing depends on the stage of writing you are currently in.

Important: Do not use generative AI tools such as ChatGPT or u:ai for research. These tools do not access databases and may fabricate sources. Instead, use dedicated literature research tools such as Elicit, Consensus or others.

The library offers specialised training sessions on [literature research with AI](http://urise.univie.ac.at/course/index.php?categoryid=6&wbtsearch=ai+literacy).

selected.title: Citing and labelling AI
heading_path: References within the text
selected.text: historical documents written in Kurrent script. All of the aforementioned applications were used exclusively in a supportive capacity and in agreement with the thesis supervisor. Their use was in accordance with the applicable AI guidelines of the University of Vienna and with due regard for academic independence: they served to facilitate linguistic, technical and practical work steps and did not replace independent academic work. All results constituted solely technical or linguistic support and were critically reviewed, adapted and contextualised by the author. The selection, analysis and interpretation of sources, the development of the argument, the substantive evaluation of the data and the final drafting of the text were carried out independently. The author bears sole responsibility for all content, translations and results of this thesis. The use of AI-supported tools is therefore to be understood not as a delegation of tasks, but as a reflective complement within a collaborative working process. In particular in the case of theses, be sure to clarify with your supervisor how the use of AI is to be documented.

Traceability is ensured if, in addition to the quoted text,it is recommended to indicate - the name of the AI-tool and, where applicable, the company behind it - the model used - the year of use - and the prompt that generated the text Example: Text generated by ChatGPT (OpenAI, model o1, 2024. [4] Citing and labelling AI — References in the bibliography
AI tools are not considered traditional sources, as interaction with them, similar to personal conversations, is not reproducible or openly accessible.

Reference only with publicly accessible link

According to international citation standards, AI tools should therefore only be included in a bibliography or reference list if a public URL to the respective chat can be provided. Although tools such as ChatGPT generate unique URLs for each conversation, these are generally accessible only with the user’s login credentials and are therefore not available to third parties ([The Chicago Manual of Stile Online](https://www.chicagomanualofstyle.org/qanda/data/faq/topics/Documentation/faq0422.html)).

[5] AI in academic writing — Use Cases of AI in scientific writing
AI tools can support you at different stages of academic writing. It is important to use AI as a tool within the writing process and not as a substitute for your own subject-specific engagement. How AI may support your writing depends on the stage of writing you are currently in.

prompt_messages.content:
You are a concise assistant for prospective and current University of Vienna students. Answer the question using ONLY the numbered sources provided.
Rules:
- Base every statement on the sources; never add outside knowledge or guess.
- Cite each claim inline with the bracket number(s) of the source(s) you used, e.g. [1] or [2][3]. Cite only sources you actually relied on.
- If the sources do not contain the answer, reply that you don't know, and do NOT cite any sources.
- Keep the answer short and direct (it may be read aloud by a voice assistant).

Sources:
[1] Citing and labelling AI — References within the text

The selection, analysis and interpretation of sources, the development of the argument, the substantive evaluation of the data and the final drafting of the text were carried out independently. The author bears sole responsibility for all content, translations and results of this thesis. The use of AI-supported tools is therefore to be understood not as a delegation of tasks, but as a reflective complement within a collaborative working process. In particular in the case of theses, be sure to clarify with your supervisor how the use of AI is to be documented.

Question: What's the correct way to disclose and reference AI tools in a paper?

generate.raw_response: To disclose and reference AI tools in a paper, you should clearly distinguish the parts of the work that originate from you and indicate how AI was used [2]. For literal reproduction of AI-generated content, treat it as a conventional quotation and provide the name of the AI tool, model, year, and prompt [3]. For collaborative development, document how AI was used in principle and that you controlled, selected, and revised the content, which can be presented in a table or a single reference [1][3]. AI tools should not be included in a bibliography unless a public URL to the conversation is available [4].

answer: To disclose and reference AI tools in a paper, you should clearly distinguish the parts of the work that originate from you and indicate how AI was used [2]. For literal reproduction of AI-generated content, treat it as a conventional quotation and provide the name of the AI tool, model, year, and prompt [3]. For collaborative development, document how AI was used in principle and that you controlled, selected, and revised the content, which can be presented in a table or a single reference [1][3]. AI tools should not be included in a bibliography unless a public URL to the conversation is available [4].
